source: NIAMS + Apollo Hospitals + WHO + NHLBI + NIDDK + CDC + MedlinePlus + Government of India (NCVBDC, NHM)
source_url: https://www.niams.nih.gov/health-topics/all-diseases | https://www.who.int/news-room/fact-sheets | https://www.nhlbi.nih.gov/health | https://www.niddk.nih.gov/health-information | https://www.cdc.gov | https://medlineplus.gov | https://ncvbdc.mohfw.gov.in | https://nhm.gov.in
description: Comprehensive disease database with records from NIAMS and Apollo Hospitals, including overview, symptoms, causes, diagnosis, treatment, risk factors, prevention, and prevalence data.

diseases.name: Achilles Tendinitis
diseases.description: Achilles tendinitis is an overuse injury of the Achilles tendon, the band of tissue that connects calf muscles at the back of the lower leg to your heel bone. It most commonly occurs in runners who have suddenly increased the intensity or duration of their runs. It's also common in middle-aged people who play sports, such as tennis or basketball, only on the weekends.
symptoms: Pain and stiffness along the Achilles tendon in the morning
Pain along the tendon or back of the heel that worsens with activity
Severe pain the day after exercising
Swelling that is present all the time and gets worse throughout the day with activity
causes: Sudden increase in the amount or intensity of exercise
risk_factors: Medical conditions such as psoriasis or high blood pressure
diseases.prevalence: Achilles tendinitis is very common, especially in runners and athletes.

diseases.name: Acid Reflux (GERD)
diseases.description: GERD is a long-term condition in which stomach contents flow back into the esophagus, causing heartburn and other symptoms.
symptoms: Regurgitation of food or sour liquid
causes: Weak or relaxed lower esophageal sphincter
Large meals or lying down after eating
prevention: Do not lie down right after meals
diseases.prevalence: GERD is a common digestive condition worldwide.

diseases.name: Acne
diseases.description: Acne is a chronic skin condition that occurs when hair follicles become plugged with oil and dead skin cells. It causes whiteheads, blackheads, pimples, nodules, and cysts. Acne commonly appears on the face, forehead, chest, upper back, and shoulders. While it primarily affects teenagers, it can occur at any age.
causes: Hair follicles clogged by sebum and dead skin cells
diagnosis: Skin culture if bacterial infection suspected
treatment: Topical treatments (benzoyl peroxide, salicylic acid, retinoids, antibiotics)
diseases.prevalence: Affects approximately 9.4% of the global population; up to 85% of young people aged 12–24 experience acne.

diseases.name: Allergic Rhinitis
diseases.description: Allergic rhinitis is inflammation of the nose caused by allergens such as pollen, dust, or animal dander.
diseases.prevalence: Allergic rhinitis is common and often seasonal.

diseases.name: Alopecia Areata
diseases.description: Alopecia areata is an autoimmune disease that causes hair loss on the scalp, face, and sometimes other areas of the body. The immune system mistakenly attacks hair follicles, disrupting the normal hair formation cycle. The condition can affect anyone regardless of age or gender, but often begins in childhood.
symptoms: Hair loss on face (beard, eyebrows, eyelashes)
Hair loss may affect entire scalp (alopecia totalis) or entire body (alopecia universalis)
Tingling or itching before hair loss
diagnosis: Blood tests (to rule out thyroid or other autoimmune conditions)
diseases.prevalence: About 6.8 million people in the United States have alopecia areata; lifetime risk approximately 2%.

diseases.name: Anemia
diseases.description: Anemia (anaemia) is a condition in which the number of red blood cells or the hemoglobin they contain is lower than normal, reducing the blood's ability to carry oxygen to tissues.
symptoms: Pale skin or pale inner eyelids
causes: Iron deficiency from poor intake or absorption
Blood loss (heavy menstrual bleeding or bleeding from the gut)
Infections such as malaria or other chronic infections
Chronic diseases or inherited blood disorders
diagnosis: Complete blood count (CBC) and hemoglobin level
Evaluation for blood loss or infection
Assessment for chronic disease or inherited conditions
treatment: Iron, folate, or vitamin B12 supplementation when needed
Treatment of infections or chronic disease
prevention: Eat a diverse diet with iron-rich foods
Use supplements when advised by a clinician
Prevent and treat infections such as malaria
Seek care for heavy menstrual bleeding
diseases.prevalence: Anemia is a major public health concern worldwide, especially among children and women in low- and middle-income countries.

diseases.name: Ankylosing Spondylitis
diseases.description: Ankylosing spondylitis is a type of arthritis that primarily affects the spine. It causes inflammation of the spinal joints (vertebrae) that can lead to severe, chronic pain and discomfort. In more advanced cases, this inflammation can cause new bone to form on the spine, causing the spine to fuse in a fixed, immobile position, sometimes creating a hunched-forward posture.
symptoms: Pain and stiffness in the lower back and hips, especially in the morning and after periods of inactivity
Pain and swelling in other joints such as the shoulders, hips, knees, and feet
causes: Unknown, but genetic factors are involved
HLA-B27 gene is present in most people with this condition
prevention: Exercise and physical therapy can help manage symptoms
diseases.prevalence: Ankylosing spondylitis affects about 0.1% to 0.5% of the adult population.

diseases.name: Antiphospholipid Syndrome (APS)
diseases.description: Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) is an autoimmune disorder in which the immune system produces abnormal antibodies against phospholipids — a type of fat — in the blood. APS can cause blood clots to form in arteries and veins, as well as pregnancy complications such as miscarriages and preterm births. It may occur alone or with another autoimmune disease such as lupus.
symptoms: Lacy, net-like purplish skin rash (livedo reticularis)
causes: Autoimmune antibodies (antiphospholipid antibodies: anticardiolipin, anti-β2 glycoprotein I, lupus anticoagulant)
May be triggered by infections, medications
diagnosis: Blood tests for antiphospholipid antibodies (twice at least 12 weeks apart)
risk_factors: Having lupus or another autoimmune disease
diseases.prevalence: APS occurs in about 1–5% of the general population; seen in 15–20% of people with recurrent miscarriages.

diseases.name: Arthritis
diseases.description: Arthritis is a broad term that refers to inflammation of one or more joints, causing pain, swelling, stiffness, and limited range of motion. There are more than 100 different types of arthritis. The most common forms are osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis. Arthritis is a leading cause of disability in the United States.
symptoms: Swelling in one or more joints
Stiffness in the morning or after sitting still
Redness of the skin around the joint
causes: Depends on the type: cartilage breakdown (OA), autoimmune attack (RA), uric acid crystals (gout)
diagnosis: Blood tests (rheumatoid factor, ANA, ESR, CRP)
diseases.prevalence: Over 54 million adults in the United States have doctor-diagnosed arthritis.

diseases.name: Asthma
diseases.description: Asthma is a chronic condition that inflames and narrows the airways, making it harder to breathe. Symptoms can come and go and are often triggered by infections, allergens, or exercise.
symptoms: Coughing at night or early morning
treatment: Reliever inhalers for quick symptom relief
Controller medicines such as inhaled corticosteroids
risk_factors: Family history of asthma or allergies
Exposure to tobacco smoke or pollution
Allergic conditions such as eczema or rhinitis
prevention: Avoid tobacco smoke and indoor pollutants
Get timely care for worsening symptoms
diseases.prevalence: Asthma is common worldwide and often begins in childhood, but can affect people of any age.

diseases.name: Avascular Necrosis (Osteonecrosis)
diseases.description: Avascular necrosis, also called osteonecrosis, is bone death caused by reduced blood flow to the bone. When blood doesn't reach bone tissue, the bone begins to die and may eventually collapse. The hip is the most commonly affected joint, but avascular necrosis can also occur in the knee, shoulder, ankle, and wrist.
symptoms: Pain in the joint that may be mild at first and progressively worsens
Pain only when putting weight on the affected joint
Groin pain spreading to knee or buttock
causes: Trauma or injury to the joint
Blood disorders such as sickle cell disease
treatment: Medications to relieve pain and prevent bone loss
risk_factors: Medical conditions: sickle cell, lupus, Gaucher disease
diseases.prevalence: Approximately 10,000 to 20,000 new cases occur each year in the United States.

diseases.name: Back Pain
diseases.description: Back pain is one of the most common medical problems, affecting most people at some point in their lives. It can range from a dull, constant ache to a sudden, sharp sensation that leaves a person incapacitated. Pain can be classified as acute (less than 6 weeks), sub-chronic (6–12 weeks), or chronic (more than 12 weeks). Most back pain is mechanical in nature.
symptoms: Pain that radiates down your leg
Pain that worsens with bending, lifting, standing, or walking
diseases.prevalence: Back pain is the leading cause of disability worldwide, with nearly 80% of adults experiencing it at some point.

diseases.name: Behçet's Disease
diseases.description: Behçet's disease is a rare disorder that causes blood vessel inflammation throughout the body. This can lead to numerous signs and symptoms that can seem unrelated at first. The disease can cause mouth sores, eye inflammation, skin rashes, and lesions, and genital sores. It is also known as Behçet's syndrome or Silk Road disease.
diseases.prevalence: Rare in Western countries; prevalence ranges from 1 per million in the UK to 420 per 100,000 in Turkey.

diseases.name: Bronchitis
diseases.description: Bronchitis is inflammation of the bronchial tubes, often causing cough with mucus. It can be acute (short-term) or chronic.
causes: Exposure to smoke or air pollution
Irritants such as dust or fumes
diseases.prevalence: Acute bronchitis is common after viral infections.

diseases.name: Bursitis
diseases.description: Bursitis is a painful condition that affects the small, fluid-filled sacs called bursae that cushion the bones, tendons, and muscles near joints. Bursitis occurs when bursae become inflamed. The most common locations are the shoulder, elbow, and hip. Bursitis often occurs near joints that perform frequent repetitive motion.
symptoms: Pain that worsens with movement or pressure
treatment: Rest and avoiding activities that worsen pain
risk_factors: Occupations or hobbies with repetitive movements
prevention: Warm up and stretch before activity
Use knee pads or elbow pads
diseases.prevalence: Common condition affecting millions of people worldwide.

diseases.name: Carpal Tunnel Syndrome
diseases.description: Carpal tunnel syndrome is a common condition that causes pain, numbness, and tingling in the hand and arm. The condition occurs when one of the major nerves to the hand — the median nerve — is squeezed or compressed as it travels through a narrow passageway in the wrist called the carpal tunnel.
symptoms: Numbness, tingling, or pain in the thumb, index, middle, and ring fingers
Occasional shock-like sensations that radiate to the thumb and index, middle, and ring fingers
Pain or tingling that may travel up the forearm toward the shoulder
Weakness and clumsiness in the hand
causes: Anything that squeezes or irritates the median nerve in the carpal tunnel space
diseases.prevalence: Affects approximately 3–6% of adults in the general population.

diseases.name: Chickenpox (Varicella)
diseases.description: Chickenpox is a highly contagious infection caused by the varicella-zoster virus, marked by an itchy blister-like rash.
causes: Spread through respiratory droplets or direct contact
diagnosis: Clinical assessment based on rash pattern
treatment: Symptom relief for fever and itching
diseases.prevalence: Chickenpox remains common where vaccination coverage is incomplete.

diseases.name: Chikungunya
diseases.description: Chikungunya is a mosquito-borne viral illness that causes sudden fever and severe joint pain. Joint pain can be debilitating and may last for weeks or longer.
diagnosis: Blood tests such as RT-PCR or antibody testing
treatment: Fever and pain control with paracetamol
risk_factors: Living in or traveling to outbreak areas
prevention: Eliminate standing water to reduce breeding
diseases.prevalence: Chikungunya occurs in many parts of Asia, including India, with periodic outbreaks.

diseases.name: Childhood Arthritis (Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis)
diseases.description: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA), formerly known as juvenile rheumatoid arthritis, is the most common type of arthritis in children under 16. JIA can cause persistent joint pain, swelling, and stiffness. Some children may experience symptoms for only a few months, while others have symptoms for the rest of their lives.
symptoms: Pain, swelling, and stiffness in joints
treatment: Eye drops or surgery for uveitis
risk_factors: Age between 2–3 years or early teens
diseases.prevalence: Affects approximately 1 in 1,000 children in the United States.

diseases.name: Cholera
diseases.description: Cholera is an acute diarrheal infection caused by Vibrio cholerae from contaminated food or water. It can cause severe dehydration if not treated quickly.
diagnosis: Stool testing or rapid diagnostic tests
prevention: Oral cholera vaccine in at-risk areas
diseases.prevalence: Cholera outbreaks occur in areas with limited access to safe water and sanitation.

diseases.name: Chondromalacia Patella (Runner's Knee)
diseases.description: Chondromalacia patella refers to the softening and breakdown of the cartilage on the underside of the kneecap (patella). It is one of the most common causes of chronic knee pain, especially in young athletes. The condition develops when the kneecap rubs abnormally against the thigh bone, causing irritation of the cartilage.
symptoms: Dull aching pain around or behind the kneecap
Pain that worsens when climbing stairs, kneeling, or squatting
Pain after sitting for a long time with bent knees
Grinding or cracking sound when the knee is flexed
causes: Flat feet causing inward knee rotation
diseases.prevalence: One of the most common knee conditions; affects up to 25% of active young adults.

diseases.name: Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)
diseases.description: Chronic kidney disease means the kidneys are damaged and cannot filter blood properly over time.
treatment: Dialysis or transplant in advanced disease
prevention: Manage blood pressure and blood sugar
diseases.prevalence: CKD is common and often detected through routine testing.

diseases.name: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD)
diseases.description: COPD is a chronic lung disease that causes airflow limitation and breathing difficulty, commonly due to long-term exposure to lung irritants such as tobacco smoke.
symptoms: Shortness of breath, especially with activity
causes: Exposure to air pollution, dust, or chemical fumes
diagnosis: Assessment of exposure to lung irritants
treatment: Smoking cessation and avoidance of irritants
risk_factors: Indoor or outdoor air pollution exposure
History of asthma or lung infections
prevention: Do not smoke and avoid secondhand smoke
Reduce exposure to air pollution and workplace fumes
Use protective equipment in dusty environments
Keep vaccinations up to date (flu, pneumonia) when advised
diseases.prevalence: COPD is a common chronic lung disease and is often underdiagnosed, especially in adults over 40.

diseases.name: Complex Regional Pain Syndrome (CRPS)
diseases.description: Complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS) is a chronic pain condition that most often affects a limb (arm, leg, hand, or foot) after an injury. CRPS is characterized by prolonged or excessive pain and changes in skin color, temperature, and/or swelling in the affected area. There are two types: CRPS-I (without confirmed nerve injury) and CRPS-II (with confirmed nerve injury, formerly called causalgia).
symptoms: Changes in skin temperature (alternately sweaty and cold)
Changes in skin color (blotchy, purple, pale, or red)
causes: Abnormal interaction between central and peripheral nervous systems
In some cases, no clear precipitating event
prevention: Vitamin C after limb fracture may reduce risk
Prompt treatment of pain after injury
diseases.prevalence: Estimated 200,000 new cases per year in the United States; affects approximately 1–2 per 100,000 people annually.

diseases.name: Coronary Artery Disease (CAD)
diseases.description: Coronary artery disease occurs when the arteries that supply oxygen-rich blood to the heart become narrowed by plaque buildup, reducing blood flow to the heart muscle.
symptoms: Pain in neck, jaw, shoulder, or back
Symptoms may be absent until a heart attack
diagnosis: Echocardiography or coronary imaging when needed
treatment: Medicines for cholesterol, blood pressure, or clot prevention
Procedures such as angioplasty and stenting
risk_factors: High blood pressure or high cholesterol
prevention: Control blood pressure, cholesterol, and diabetes
diseases.prevalence: Coronary artery disease is the most common type of heart disease and a leading cause of illness worldwide.

diseases.name: COVID-19
diseases.description: COVID-19 is a respiratory infection caused by the SARS-CoV-2 coronavirus. It spreads mainly through respiratory particles in the air and can range from mild illness to severe disease.
symptoms: Loss or change of taste or smell
causes: Close contact in crowded or poorly ventilated spaces
treatment: Antiviral medicines for high-risk patients when indicated
Oxygen therapy or hospital care for severe disease
risk_factors: Chronic diseases such as diabetes or heart disease
prevention: Good ventilation and mask use in crowded settings
diseases.prevalence: COVID-19 continues to circulate globally with periodic outbreaks.

diseases.name: De Quervain's Tenosynovitis
diseases.description: De Quervain's tenosynovitis is a painful condition affecting the tendons on the thumb side of the wrist. It occurs when the two tendons associated with thumb movement become irritated or inflamed within the narrow tunnel they pass through. This condition is common in new mothers and people whose activities involve repetitive hand and wrist motions.
symptoms: Pain and swelling near the base of the thumb
Sticking or stop-and-go sensation when moving the thumb
Pain worsening when turning the wrist, grasping, or making a fist
causes: Direct injury to the wrist or tendon
Inflammatory arthritis such as rheumatoid arthritis
diseases.prevalence: Affects approximately 0.5% of men and 1.3% of women annually.

diseases.name: Dehydration
diseases.description: Dehydration happens when the body loses more fluid than it takes in, affecting normal body function.
prevention: Replace fluids during illness or heat
diseases.prevalence: Dehydration is common during hot weather and diarrheal illnesses.

diseases.name: Dengue Fever
diseases.description: Dengue fever is a viral infection transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes, common in tropical and subtropical regions. It can range from mild illness to severe dengue.
diagnosis: Blood tests such as antigen or PCR testing
treatment: Fever and pain control with paracetamol
Avoid NSAIDs that increase bleeding risk
risk_factors: Living or traveling in tropical areas
Previous dengue infection (risk of severe dengue)
diseases.prevalence: Dengue is common in tropical and subtropical regions with seasonal outbreaks.

diseases.name: Dermatomyositis
diseases.description: Dermatomyositis is an uncommon inflammatory disease characterized by muscle weakness and a distinctive skin rash. It belongs to the group of inflammatory myopathies. The disease can affect adults and children. In adults, dermatomyositis may be associated with a higher risk of cancer. In children, it causes skin changes and muscle weakness.
symptoms: Skin rash (heliotrope rash on eyelids, Gottron's papules on knuckles)
Hardening skin deposits under skin (calcinosis) in children
causes: Autoimmune attack on muscle and skin blood vessels
diseases.prevalence: Rare; incidence of 1–10 per million per year.

diseases.name: Diabetes Mellitus
diseases.description: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic condition where blood glucose levels are too high because the body does not make enough insulin or cannot use it properly. Type 2 diabetes is the most common form.
causes: Insulin resistance or reduced insulin production
diagnosis: Oral glucose tolerance test when needed
treatment: Healthy diet and regular physical activity
prevention: Healthy diet with less sugar and saturated fat
diseases.prevalence: Diabetes is common and rising in India and globally, with type 2 diabetes accounting for most cases.

diseases.name: Diarrhoeal Disease
diseases.description: Diarrhoeal disease is usually caused by infection of the intestinal tract and is commonly spread through contaminated food or drinking water or poor hygiene.
symptoms: Blood in stool in severe cases
diagnosis: Stool testing in severe or prolonged cases
diseases.prevalence: Diarrhoeal disease is a leading cause of illness in children and remains a major public health concern.

diseases.name: Diphtheria
diseases.description: Diphtheria is a serious bacterial infection that can affect the throat or skin and produce a toxin that causes severe illness.
symptoms: Gray coating in throat or nose
diseases.prevalence: Diphtheria is rare where vaccination coverage is high but still occurs in outbreaks.

diseases.name: Discoid Lupus Erythematosus (DLE)
diseases.description: Discoid lupus erythematosus (DLE) is a chronic inflammatory skin condition that causes scarring skin lesions. It is the most common form of cutaneous (skin) lupus. Lesions are typically round, scaly, and red-to-violet in color and can leave permanent scars. Although primarily a skin condition, a small percentage of DLE patients may develop systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE).
symptoms: Round, coin-shaped, scaly plaques on the face, scalp, ears, and neck
Skin discoloration (hyperpigmentation or hypopigmentation) after lesions heal
Scarring hair loss (scarring alopecia) on the scalp
causes: UV light triggers or worsens lesions
diagnosis: ANA and other lupus antibody testing (to rule out SLE)
diseases.prevalence: Affects approximately 3 per 100,000 people; DLE is the most common form of cutaneous lupus.

diseases.name: Dupuytren's Contracture
diseases.description: Dupuytren's contracture is a hand deformity that usually develops over years, in which the tissue beneath the skin of the palm — the fascia — thickens and tightens over time, eventually causing one or more fingers to curl inward. It most commonly affects the ring finger and little finger and can make it difficult to use the hand fully.
symptoms: Firm lumps or nodules in the palm
Cords of tissue forming under skin of palm
Fingers bending toward palm and unable to fully straighten
Difficulty picking up or grasping objects
diseases.prevalence: Affects approximately 5% of Americans, more common in men over 50.

diseases.name: Dyslipidemia (High Cholesterol)
diseases.description: Dyslipidemia means unhealthy levels of blood fats (cholesterol and triglycerides), which increases the risk of heart disease and stroke.
causes: Unhealthy diet high in saturated or trans fats
diseases.prevalence: High cholesterol is common and often detected on routine blood tests.

diseases.name: Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis)
diseases.description: Eczema, or atopic dermatitis, is a chronic inflammatory skin condition that causes the skin to become itchy, red, dry, and cracked. It is common in children but can occur at any age. Eczema tends to flare periodically, often in association with environmental triggers. It is frequently found alongside other allergic conditions such as asthma and hay fever.
symptoms: Small, raised bumps that may weep fluid when scratched
causes: Environmental triggers: soaps, detergents, dust, animal dander, certain foods, stress, sweat
diagnosis: Allergy skin test or blood test
risk_factors: Family history of eczema, asthma, or hay fever
diseases.prevalence: Affects about 31.6 million Americans (10.1%); up to 20% of children worldwide.

diseases.name: Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome (EDS)
diseases.description: Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (EDS) is a group of inherited disorders that affect the connective tissues — primarily the skin, joints, and blood vessel walls. People who have EDS usually have overly flexible joints and stretchy, fragile skin. EDS is caused by defects in collagen production, and there are 13 subtypes with varying severity.
symptoms: Skin that heals poorly leaving wide scars
causes: Mutations in genes controlling collagen production or processing
Autosomal dominant or recessive inheritance depending on type
treatment: Physical therapy to strengthen muscles and protect joints
Surgery for severe joint or vascular complications
risk_factors: Female sex (more commonly diagnosed in women)
prevention: Joint protection strategies help prevent injury
diseases.prevalence: EDS affects approximately 1 in 2,500–5,000 people. Hypermobile EDS is the most common subtype.

diseases.name: Fatty Liver Disease (NAFLD)
diseases.description: Fatty liver disease occurs when excess fat builds up in the liver, often linked to obesity, diabetes, and metabolic syndrome.
symptoms: Discomfort in the upper right abdomen
diseases.prevalence: Fatty liver disease is increasingly common alongside obesity and diabetes.

diseases.name: Fibromyalgia
diseases.description: Fibromyalgia is a disorder characterized by widespread musculoskeletal pain accompanied by fatigue, sleep, memory, and mood issues. Researchers believe that fibromyalgia amplifies painful sensations by affecting the way the brain and spinal cord process painful and nonpainful signals.
symptoms: Widespread pain described as a constant dull ache lasting at least three months
Tingling or numbness in hands and feet
Sensitivity to temperature, light, or sound
causes: Altered pain signaling in the brain and spinal cord
Possible triggers: infections, physical or emotional trauma
diagnosis: ACR criteria: widespread pain for 3+ months with no underlying condition
prevention: Regular exercise and stress management may reduce risk
diseases.prevalence: Affects approximately 4 million US adults (about 2% of the population).

diseases.name: Flat Feet (Pes Planus)
diseases.description: Flat feet (pes planus) is a condition in which the arch of the foot is lower than normal or entirely absent, causing the entire sole of the foot to make contact with the ground. Flat feet can be present from birth or may develop over time. Most people with flat feet experience no pain; however, some develop foot, ankle, knee, or back pain.
symptoms: Foot pain, especially in the arch or heel
The inside bottom of the foot touching the ground
Tired or achy feet after standing
Swelling on the inside of the ankle
Knee, hip, or back pain due to altered alignment
causes: Fallen arches over time (posterior tibial tendon dysfunction)
Injury to the foot or ankle
diagnosis: Physical examination (wet footprint test, tiptoe test)
prevention: Avoid high heels for prolonged periods
diseases.prevalence: Affects about 20–30% of adults; most have no symptoms.

diseases.name: Food Poisoning (Gastroenteritis)
diseases.description: Food poisoning (acute gastroenteritis) is illness caused by eating or drinking contaminated food or water. It often leads to diarrhea and vomiting.
diagnosis: Stool tests for severe or prolonged illness
diseases.prevalence: Foodborne illness is common worldwide and affects all age groups.

diseases.name: Fractures (Bone Fractures)
diseases.description: A bone fracture is a break in the continuity of a bone. Fractures can range from a thin crack to a complete break, and may be caused by high-force impact, stress, or weakening of the bone by conditions such as osteoporosis or cancer. They can occur in any bone in the body and are one of the most common musculoskeletal injuries.
symptoms: Sudden onset pain at the time of injury
Inability to use the affected area
causes: Pathological fractures (bone weakened by disease: osteoporosis, tumor)
diseases.prevalence: Approximately 6.8 million fractures occur in the United States each year.

diseases.name: Giant Cell Arteritis (Temporal Arteritis)
diseases.description: Giant cell arteritis is an inflammation of the blood vessels, especially the arteries that supply the head. It most often causes headaches, jaw pain, and vision problems. Immediate treatment is needed to prevent blindness. Giant cell arteritis often occurs with polymyalgia rheumatica. It primarily affects people over 50.
symptoms: Persistent, severe headache usually in the temple area
Vision problems (double vision or vision loss)
Shoulder and hip pain (if also PMR)
causes: Autoimmune inflammation of large and medium blood vessel walls
treatment: High-dose corticosteroids (prednisone) — must start immediately to prevent vision loss
diseases.prevalence: Affects about 200 per 100,000 persons over age 50 in the United States.

diseases.name: Golfer's Elbow (Medial Epicondylitis)
diseases.description: Golfer's elbow (medial epicondylitis) is a painful condition caused by overuse of the forearm muscles and tendons that control wrist and finger movements. It causes pain and inflammation at the medial epicondyle — the bony bump on the inside of the elbow. Despite the name, it affects more than just golfers.
symptoms: Pain and tenderness on the inner side of the elbow
Weakness in the hands and wrists
Numbness or tingling radiating to the ring and little fingers
Pain worsens with specific movements (swinging, squeezing, turning)
causes: Repetitive motions of the wrist and fingers (swinging a golf club, throwing, using tools)
Damage to the muscles and tendons at the medial epicondyle
diseases.prevalence: Affects approximately 1–3% of adults; accounts for 10–20% of all epicondylitis cases.

diseases.name: Gout
diseases.description: Gout is a common and complex form of arthritis that can affect anyone. It's characterized by sudden, severe attacks of pain, swelling, redness, and tenderness in one or more joints, most often in the big toe. Gout occurs when urate crystals accumulate in your joint, causing the inflammation and intense pain of a gout attack.
symptoms: Intense joint pain (most severe in the first 4–12 hours)
causes: Diet high in purines (red meat, shellfish)
diagnosis: Joint fluid analysis (most definitive — urate crystals)
treatment: Medications to lower uric acid (allopurinol, febuxostat)
diseases.prevalence: Gout affects approximately 9.2 million adults in the United States.

diseases.name: Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (Wegener's)
diseases.description: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA), formerly called Wegener's granulomatosis, is a rare disorder that causes inflammation of the blood vessels in the nose, sinuses, throat, lungs, and kidneys. It belongs to the group of vasculitis disorders. Untreated, GPA can be life-threatening due to organ damage.
treatment: TMP-SMX for localized disease and infection prevention
diseases.prevalence: Rare; affects approximately 3 per 100,000 people in the United States.

diseases.name: Hair Disorders
diseases.description: Hair disorders encompass a variety of conditions affecting hair growth, structure, and scalp health. These include androgenetic alopecia (male/female pattern baldness), telogen effluvium, trichotillomania, and tinea capitis among others. Hair loss affects millions of people and can have significant psychological impact.
symptoms: Gradual thinning on top of head
Patches of scaling that spread over the scalp
causes: Medical conditions (alopecia areata, thyroid disease, iron deficiency)
diseases.prevalence: Androgenetic alopecia affects about 50 million men and 30 million women in the United States.

diseases.name: Heat Stroke
diseases.description: Heat stroke is the most severe heat-related illness and occurs when the body can no longer control its temperature.
symptoms: Hot, dry skin or heavy sweating
risk_factors: Older age or very young children
Outdoor work or sports in heat
prevention: Avoid outdoor activity during peak heat
diseases.prevalence: Heat stroke risk rises during extreme heat waves and hot seasons.

diseases.name: Hemophilic Arthropathy
diseases.description: Hemophilic arthropathy is progressive joint damage resulting from repeated bleeding into the joints (hemarthrosis) in people with hemophilia. Repeated blood accumulation in joint cavities leads to chronic synovitis, cartilage degradation, and joint destruction. The knees, ankles, and elbows are most commonly affected.
symptoms: Swollen, warm, painful joints after bleeding episode
causes: Repeated hemarthrosis (joint bleeding) in hemophilia A or B
Iron from blood deposits damages cartilage and synovium
diagnosis: Clinical history of hemophilia with joint problems
prevention: Prophylactic clotting factor replacement starting in early childhood
diseases.prevalence: Affects virtually all patients with severe hemophilia who do not receive prophylaxis; ~30,000 Americans have hemophilia.

diseases.name: Hepatitis A
diseases.description: Hepatitis A is a liver infection caused by the hepatitis A virus, usually spread through contaminated food or water or close contact with an infected person.
causes: Ingestion of contaminated food or water
Close contact with an infected person
diagnosis: Blood test for HAV IgM antibodies
treatment: Supportive care with rest and hydration
Avoid alcohol and unnecessary liver-toxic medicines
diseases.prevalence: Hepatitis A occurs worldwide and is more common where sanitation and safe water access are limited.

diseases.name: Hepatitis B
diseases.description: Hepatitis B is a liver infection caused by the hepatitis B virus, spread through blood or body fluids. It can become chronic and lead to serious liver disease.
diagnosis: Blood tests for hepatitis B markers (HBsAg, anti-HBs)
treatment: Antiviral therapy for chronic hepatitis B when indicated
risk_factors: Exposure to blood or body fluids
Household contact with an infected person
prevention: Safe injection practices and screened blood
diseases.prevalence: Hepatitis B remains a major global health problem, especially in parts of Asia and Africa.

diseases.name: Hepatitis E
diseases.description: Hepatitis E is a liver infection caused by the hepatitis E virus, commonly spread through contaminated water.
diagnosis: Blood tests for hepatitis E antibodies
diseases.prevalence: Hepatitis E is common in many low- and middle-income regions, including South Asia.

diseases.name: Hip Replacement Surgery
diseases.description: Hip replacement surgery is a procedure in which a doctor surgically removes a painful hip joint with arthritis and replaces it with an artificial joint often made from metal and plastic components. It usually is done when all other treatment options have failed to provide adequate pain relief.
symptoms: Severe hip pain that limits everyday activities
Hip pain that continues while resting, even at night
Stiffness in the hip that limits movement
Significant side effects from pain medication
diseases.prevalence: More than 450,000 total hip replacements are performed annually in the United States.

diseases.name: Hypermobility Spectrum Disorder (HSD)
diseases.description: Hypermobility Spectrum Disorder (HSD) is a group of conditions characterized by joint hypermobility that causes pain and functional impairment, but does not meet the diagnostic criteria for a heritable connective tissue disorder such as Ehlers-Danlos syndrome. HSD is classified into four subtypes based on the distribution of hypermobility and associated features.
causes: Abnormalities in collagen structure or processing
diagnosis: Assessment of pain and functional impairment
Exclusion of defined connective tissue disorders
prevention: Protective exercise strategies reduce injury risk
diseases.prevalence: Prevalence estimated at 0.75–2% with symptomatic hypermobility; much higher rates of asymptomatic hypermobility.

diseases.name: Hypertension
diseases.description: Hypertension is persistently high blood pressure. It often has no symptoms but increases the risk of heart disease, stroke, and kidney problems.
symptoms: Chest pain or shortness of breath
diagnosis: Blood pressure measurements on at least two occasions
Assessment for related risk factors and organ effects
treatment: Medications such as ACE inhibitors or calcium channel blockers
diseases.prevalence: Hypertension is common worldwide and often undiagnosed without regular blood pressure checks.

diseases.name: Inclusion Body Myositis (IBM)
diseases.description: Inclusion body myositis (IBM) is the most common acquired muscle disease in adults over 50. It is a slowly progressive muscle disease characterized by both inflammatory and degenerative features. Unlike other inflammatory myopathies, IBM is relatively resistant to immunosuppressive therapy. It typically affects finger flexors and quadriceps muscles first.
symptoms: Slowly progressive weakness in finger flexors (difficulty gripping)
Weakness in the quadriceps (difficulty getting up from a chair or climbing stairs)
causes: Protein aggregates within muscle fibers (amyloid deposits)
diseases.prevalence: Estimated incidence 1–71 per million per year; increases significantly with age.

diseases.name: Inflammatory Arthritis
diseases.description: Inflammatory arthritis is a term used to describe a group of conditions caused by an overactive immune system that attacks the joints, leading to inflammation, pain, swelling, and potential joint damage. It includes rheumatoid arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, and other conditions. Unlike osteoarthritis (wear-and-tear), inflammatory arthritis is systemic.
prevention: Not smoking reduces risk of RA
diseases.prevalence: Estimated 3–4 million Americans have some form of inflammatory arthritis.

diseases.name: Inflammatory Bowel Disease-Related Arthritis
diseases.description: Inflammatory arthritis associated with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) — including Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis — is one of the most common extraintestinal manifestations of IBD. Joint inflammation can affect peripheral joints (often paralleling bowel disease activity) or the spine and sacroiliac joints (which often runs an independent course).
symptoms: Joint pain and swelling (often lower extremities)
Symptoms may correlate with IBD flares
causes: Autoimmune — shared inflammatory pathways between gut and joints
Gut bacteria translocating and triggering immune response
diagnosis: Clinical evaluation in context of IBD diagnosis
X-ray or MRI of spine and sacroiliac joints
treatment: Treating underlying IBD (TNF inhibitors helpful for both)
prevention: No specific prevention; controlling IBD may reduce joint manifestations
diseases.prevalence: Peripheral arthritis in 5–20% of IBD patients; axial disease in 3–10%.

diseases.name: Influenza (Seasonal)
diseases.description: Seasonal influenza (flu) is an acute respiratory infection caused by influenza viruses and spreads easily from person to person.
diagnosis: Rapid flu tests or PCR when needed
diseases.prevalence: Seasonal influenza causes widespread illness each year worldwide.

diseases.name: Interstitial Lung Disease (ILD) in Rheumatic Diseases
diseases.description: Interstitial lung disease (ILD) in the context of rheumatic diseases refers to lung scarring and inflammation that occurs as a complication of autoimmune conditions such as scleroderma, rheumatoid arthritis, myositis, or Sjögren's syndrome. The inflammation can lead to progressive scarring (fibrosis), reducing the ability to breathe.
causes: Autoimmune-driven inflammation in the lung tissue
Associated conditions: scleroderma, RA, myositis, Sjögren's, MCTD
diseases.prevalence: Occurs in up to 80% of scleroderma patients; about 10% of RA patients develop significant ILD.

diseases.name: Iron Deficiency Anemia
diseases.description: Iron deficiency anemia occurs when the body does not have enough iron to make healthy red blood cells.
causes: Blood loss (heavy periods or GI bleeding)
diseases.prevalence: Iron deficiency anemia is the most common form of anemia.

diseases.name: Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS)
diseases.description: IBS is a chronic gut-brain interaction disorder that causes abdominal pain and changes in bowel habits.
diagnosis: Tests to rule out other conditions
diseases.prevalence: IBS is a common chronic digestive disorder.

diseases.name: Japanese Encephalitis
diseases.description: Japanese encephalitis is a mosquito-borne viral infection that can cause inflammation of the brain. Most infections are mild, but severe disease can be life-threatening.
diagnosis: Laboratory testing (blood or CSF) when available
prevention: Mosquito bite prevention and vector control
diseases.prevalence: Japanese encephalitis is endemic in parts of Asia, including India, with seasonal transmission.

diseases.name: Joint Hypermobility Syndrome
diseases.description: Joint hypermobility syndrome is a condition in which the joints are unusually flexible and have a wider range of motion than normal. While hypermobile joints are common in children and can be advantageous for gymnasts and dancers, in hypermobility syndrome the excessive flexibility causes pain and other symptoms. It is closely related to hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome.
causes: Connective tissue laxity likely due to collagen abnormalities
prevention: Protective strategies during activity reduce injury
diseases.prevalence: Affects approximately 10–15% of the general population to some degree.

diseases.name: Kala-azar (Visceral Leishmaniasis)
diseases.description: Kala-azar is the most severe form of leishmaniasis. It is caused by parasites transmitted by sandflies and can be fatal if left untreated.
prevention: Early diagnosis and treatment to reduce transmission
diseases.prevalence: Visceral leishmaniasis remains endemic in parts of India and other regions of Asia, Africa, and the Americas.

diseases.name: Keloids and Hypertrophic Scars
diseases.description: Keloids are raised overgrowths of scar tissue that occur at the site of a skin injury. Unlike hypertrophic scars, keloids grow beyond the original wound boundaries and do not regress over time. They result from an overproduction of collagen during wound healing. Keloids can cause discomfort and cosmetic concerns.
causes: Abnormal wound healing with excessive collagen production
Triggered by skin injury: surgery, acne, piercings, burns, vaccination sites
prevention: Avoid unnecessary skin procedures in predisposed individuals
diseases.prevalence: Keloids occur in about 10% of people; much higher rates (up to 16%) in those of African or Asian descent.

diseases.name: Knee Problems
diseases.description: The knee is the largest joint in the body. Knee problems are very common, and they occur in people of all ages. Knee problems can interfere with many things, from participation in sports to simply getting up from a chair and walking. Many types of minor knee problems can be treated with self-care and do not require medical treatment.
symptoms: Redness and warmth to the touch
Inability to fully straighten the knee
causes: Sports or exercise injuries (ACL, meniscus tears)
risk_factors: Lack of muscle flexibility or strength
diseases.prevalence: Knee pain is the second most common cause of chronic pain and affects over 100 million people worldwide.

diseases.name: Leptospirosis
diseases.description: Leptospirosis is a bacterial disease spread through contact with water or soil contaminated with urine from infected animals, often after heavy rains or floods.
causes: Exposure to contaminated floodwater or soil
diseases.prevalence: Leptospirosis risk increases after heavy rainfall and flooding in tropical regions.

diseases.name: Liver Cirrhosis
diseases.description: Cirrhosis is scarring of the liver that develops over time due to chronic liver damage.
symptoms: Loss of appetite and weight loss
diseases.prevalence: Cirrhosis is a major cause of liver-related illness worldwide.

diseases.name: Low Bone Density (Osteopenia)
diseases.description: Osteopenia refers to bone mineral density that is lower than normal but not low enough to be classified as osteoporosis. People with osteopenia have weaker bones than normal and are at higher risk of developing osteoporosis and fractures. Osteopenia is a warning sign that action should be taken to improve bone health.
symptoms: May have fractures with minimal trauma
Discovered incidentally on imaging or DEXA scan
causes: Low calcium and vitamin D intake
prevention: Adequate calcium (1,000–1,200 mg/day) and vitamin D (600–800 IU/day)
diseases.prevalence: About 43 million US adults have osteopenia.

diseases.name: Lupus
diseases.description: Lupus is a chronic, autoimmune disease that can damage any part of the body, including the skin, joints, and/or organs. In lupus, something goes wrong with the immune system, which is the part of the body that fights off viruses, bacteria, and germs. The most common form of lupus is systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), which affects multiple organs.
symptoms: Skin lesions that appear or worsen with sun exposure
Fingers and toes that turn white or blue when cold (Raynaud's)
causes: Autoimmune — immune system attacks healthy tissue
risk_factors: Race/ethnicity (more common in Black, Hispanic, Asian women)
prevention: Sun protection and avoiding triggers can prevent flares
diseases.prevalence: About 1.5 million Americans live with lupus; globally an estimated 5 million people have a form of lupus.

diseases.name: Lyme Disease
diseases.description: Lyme disease is an infectious disease caused by the bacteria Borrelia burgdorferi, transmitted to humans through the bite of infected blacklegged ticks. Most cases can be treated successfully with a few weeks of antibiotics. If left untreated, infection can spread to the joints, heart, and nervous system.
symptoms: Flu-like symptoms (fever, chills, fatigue, body aches, headache)
causes: Transmitted by deer tick (Ixodes scapularis) bite
risk_factors: Spending time in wooded or grassy areas
diseases.prevalence: Approximately 30,000 cases reported annually in the US; actual numbers estimated 10x higher.

diseases.name: Malaria
diseases.description: Malaria is a life-threatening disease caused by parasites transmitted to humans through the bites of infected Anopheles mosquitoes. It is preventable and curable.
symptoms: Severe cases may cause confusion or difficulty breathing
treatment: Artemisinin-based combination therapy for many cases
risk_factors: Living in or traveling to endemic areas
diseases.prevalence: Malaria is common in tropical countries and remains a major public health burden.

diseases.name: Marfan Syndrome
diseases.description: Marfan syndrome is a genetic disorder that affects the body's connective tissue. Connective tissue holds all the body's cells, organs, and tissue together. It also plays an important role in helping the body grow and develop properly. Marfan syndrome most commonly affects the heart, eyes, blood vessels, and skeleton.
symptoms: Chest sinks in or sticks out
causes: Mutation in the FBN1 gene encoding fibrillin-1
Autosomal dominant inheritance (25% are new mutations)
treatment: Beta blockers or ARBs to protect the aorta
Back brace or surgery for scoliosis
prevention: Regular monitoring can prevent serious complications
diseases.prevalence: Marfan syndrome affects about 1 in 5,000 people worldwide.

diseases.name: Measles
diseases.description: Measles is a highly contagious viral illness that spreads through the air and can cause serious complications, especially in unvaccinated children.
symptoms: Rash that spreads over the body
causes: Airborne transmission from coughs or sneezes
diagnosis: Lab testing (IgM or PCR) when needed
treatment: Vitamin A for children when indicated
diseases.prevalence: Measles outbreaks occur where vaccination coverage is low.

diseases.name: Mixed Connective Tissue Disease (MCTD)
diseases.description: Mixed connective tissue disease (MCTD) is a rare autoimmune disorder that has overlapping features of three other connective tissue diseases: systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), systemic sclerosis (scleroderma), and polymyositis. Nearly all people with MCTD have high levels of anti-U1 RNP antibodies in their blood.
treatment: Vasodilators for Raynaud's and pulmonary hypertension
diseases.prevalence: Rare; estimated 3.8 per 100,000 population.

diseases.name: Morton's Neuroma
diseases.description: Morton's neuroma is a painful condition affecting the ball of the foot, most commonly between the third and fourth toes. It involves a thickening of the tissue around one of the nerves leading to the toes. Morton's neuroma may feel as if you are standing on a pebble in your shoe or on a fold in your sock.
symptoms: Burning pain in the ball of the foot
Pain that radiates to the toes
Feeling of standing on a pebble or fold in the sock
Numbness or tingling in the affected toes
Pain that worsens with walking or tight footwear and improves with rest
causes: Nerve compression and irritation between the metatarsal heads
diseases.prevalence: Affects approximately 1 in 3 women and 1 in 10 men over their lifetime.

diseases.name: Mumps
diseases.description: Mumps is a contagious viral infection that primarily affects the salivary glands and can cause swollen cheeks and jaw pain.
diseases.prevalence: Mumps outbreaks still occur where vaccination coverage is incomplete.

diseases.name: Myasthenia Gravis
diseases.description: Myasthenia gravis is a chronic autoimmune, neuromuscular disease that causes weakness in the skeletal muscles — the muscles your body uses for movement. It occurs when communication between nerve cells and muscles is disrupted. The hallmark is muscle weakness that improves with rest and worsens with activity.
symptoms: Eye muscle weakness (drooping eyelid — ptosis, double vision)
Facial and throat muscle weakness (difficulty speaking, chewing, swallowing)
Symptoms worsen with activity, improve with rest
causes: Autoimmune: antibodies block acetylcholine receptors (or MuSK, LRP4) at neuromuscular junction
prevention: Avoid triggers (infections, stress, certain medications) to prevent crisis
diseases.prevalence: Approximately 20 per 100,000 people in the United States have myasthenia gravis.

diseases.name: Myositis (Inflammatory Muscle Disease)
diseases.description: Myositis refers to any condition causing inflammation in muscles. The most common forms are polymyositis, dermatomyositis, and inclusion-body myositis. These conditions are autoimmune diseases in which the body's own immune system attacks healthy muscle tissue, resulting in inflammation, swelling, pain, and eventual weakness.
causes: In rare cases, medications or cancer-associated
diagnosis: Blood tests (CK, aldolase, ANA, myositis-specific antibodies)
diseases.prevalence: Myositis affects approximately 50,000 people in the United States.

diseases.name: Nail Disorders
diseases.description: Nail disorders include a wide range of conditions affecting the fingernails and toenails, including infections (onychomycosis), inflammation (paronychia), structural changes (pitting, thickening), and nail changes associated with systemic diseases such as psoriasis or lupus. Some nail changes can be indicators of systemic health conditions.
symptoms: Separation of nail from nail bed (onycholysis)
prevention: Avoid barefoot walking in public areas
diseases.prevalence: Nail fungus affects approximately 10% of the general population; up to 50% of people over 70.

diseases.name: Neck Pain
diseases.description: Neck pain is a very common complaint that can result from various causes, including poor posture, muscle strain, worn joints, nerve compression, injuries, and diseases. Most neck pain improves gradually with home treatment. If neck pain persists, doctors might recommend other treatments.
symptoms: Pain that's often worsened by holding your head in one place for long periods
Decreased ability to move your head
prevention: Take frequent breaks during desk work
diseases.prevalence: Neck pain is among the top five disorders in the United States, affecting around 10–15% of the population at any given time.

diseases.name: Neonatal Sepsis
diseases.description: Neonatal sepsis is a serious infection in newborns that can rapidly become life-threatening without early recognition and treatment.
diseases.prevalence: Neonatal infections, including sepsis, are a major cause of newborn deaths in low- and middle-income countries.

diseases.name: Nerve Entrapment Syndromes
diseases.description: Nerve entrapment syndrome occurs when a nerve is compressed or pinched by surrounding structures, leading to pain, tingling, numbness, or weakness. Common types include carpal tunnel syndrome (median nerve), cubital tunnel syndrome (ulnar nerve), and tarsal tunnel syndrome. The compression can result from repetitive motion, injury, or anatomical variations.
symptoms: Numbness and tingling in the distribution of the affected nerve
Pain (can be burning, shooting, or aching)
Weakness in muscles supplied by the nerve
Symptoms often worse at night or with specific positions
diseases.prevalence: Carpal tunnel syndrome alone affects 3–6% of adults; nerve entrapment syndromes collectively are very common.

diseases.name: Obesity
diseases.description: Obesity is a chronic condition of excess body fat that increases the risk of many health problems, including diabetes, heart disease, and joint disorders.
causes: Energy intake greater than energy expenditure
treatment: Medications or bariatric surgery for selected patients
prevention: Limit sugary drinks and ultra-processed foods
Reduce screen time and sedentary behavior
diseases.prevalence: Obesity is increasing globally and is now common in many low- and middle-income countries, including India.

diseases.name: Osteoarthritis
diseases.description: Osteoarthritis (OA) is the most common form of arthritis, affecting millions of people worldwide. It occurs when the protective cartilage that cushions the ends of bones in joints gradually wears down. Although osteoarthritis can damage any joint, the disorder most commonly affects joints in the hands, knees, hips, and spine.
symptoms: Joint stiffness most notable upon waking or after inactivity
diagnosis: Blood tests (to rule out other conditions)
diseases.prevalence: Affects over 32.5 million U.S. adults. It is the most common chronic joint condition.

diseases.name: Osteogenesis Imperfecta (Brittle Bone Disease)
diseases.description: Osteogenesis imperfecta (OI), also known as brittle bone disease, is a group of genetic disorders that primarily affect the bones. People with OI have bones that break easily, often from minor trauma or even with no apparent cause. Severity ranges from mild to severe. It is caused by mutations in genes responsible for producing type I collagen.
symptoms: Blue or grey sclera (whites of eyes)
Hearing loss in adolescence or adulthood
Loose joints and low muscle tone
causes: Mutations in COL1A1 or COL1A2 genes (most common)
Autosomal dominant inheritance in most cases
New (de novo) mutations in 25% of cases
treatment: Bisphosphonates (pamidronate, zoledronate) to increase bone density
diseases.prevalence: Affects approximately 1 in 15,000–20,000 people worldwide; about 25,000–50,000 Americans.

diseases.name: Osteomalacia (Rickets in Adults)
diseases.description: Osteomalacia is a condition in which the bones become soft and weak due to a defect in the bone-building process. In children, this condition is called rickets. It most commonly results from vitamin D deficiency, which impairs the absorption of calcium and phosphate needed for bone mineralization. Unlike osteoporosis (loss of already mineralized bone), osteomalacia is a failure to mineralize new bone.
symptoms: Muscle weakness, especially in the hip girdle
causes: Kidney or liver disease affecting vitamin D metabolism
diagnosis: Blood tests (low vitamin D, calcium, phosphate; elevated PTH, alkaline phosphatase)
risk_factors: Vitamin D deficiency (limited sun exposure, dark skin, elderly)
prevention: Adequate vitamin D and calcium intake
diseases.prevalence: Exact prevalence unknown; vitamin D deficiency (the main cause) affects approximately 1 billion people worldwide.

diseases.name: Osteoporosis
diseases.description: Osteoporosis is a bone disease that occurs when the body loses too much bone, makes too little bone, or both. As a result, bones become weak and may break from a fall or, in serious cases, from minor bumps or even from sneezing. Osteoporosis means 'porous bone.' It is the most common reason for a broken bone among older adults.
symptoms: Back pain (due to fractured or collapsed vertebra)
Bone fracture more easily than expected
causes: Bone density peaks around age 30 then declines
prevention: Adequate calcium and vitamin D intake
diseases.prevalence: About 10 million Americans have osteoporosis; another 44 million have low bone density.

diseases.name: Paget's Disease of Bone
diseases.description: Paget's disease of bone is a chronic disease that causes bones to grow larger and weaker than normal. In Paget's disease, the process of old bone being replaced by new bone is disrupted. The new bone that forms is softer and weaker. Paget's disease most commonly occurs in the pelvis, skull, spine, and legs.
symptoms: Damage to cartilage of joints adjacent to affected bone
causes: Both genetic and environmental factors (possibly viral)
SQSTM1 gene mutations in familial cases
diseases.prevalence: Affects about 1% of people over age 40 in the United States, less common in other parts of the world.

diseases.name: Patellofemoral Pain Syndrome
diseases.description: Patellofemoral pain syndrome (PFPS) is a broad term used to describe pain in the front of the knee and around the kneecap (patella). It is sometimes called 'runner's knee' or 'jumper's knee.' The pain often occurs when using stairs, kneeling, squatting, or sitting with bent knees for a long time. It is one of the most common causes of knee pain.
symptoms: Dull, aching pain in the front of the knee or around the kneecap
Pain during activities that repeatedly bend the knee
Clicking or popping sounds in the knee when climbing stairs
diseases.prevalence: One of the most common knee conditions; accounts for 25–40% of all knee problems seen in sports medicine clinics.

diseases.name: Peptic Ulcer Disease
diseases.description: Peptic ulcers are sores on the lining of the stomach or duodenum, commonly caused by H. pylori infection or NSAID use.
diseases.prevalence: Peptic ulcer disease is common and treatable.

diseases.name: Plantar Fasciitis
diseases.description: Plantar fasciitis is one of the most common causes of heel pain. It involves inflammation of a thick band of tissue that runs across the bottom of each foot and connects the heel bone to the toes (plantar fascia). It commonly causes stabbing pain that usually occurs with the first steps in the morning.
symptoms: Pain that is usually worse in the morning
Pain after prolonged standing or when getting up from sitting
Pain after — but not during — exercise
causes: Tension and stress on the plantar fascia
diagnosis: X-rays (to rule out stress fracture or bone spur)
diseases.prevalence: Plantar fasciitis affects about 2 million Americans each year and about 10% of the population over a lifetime.

diseases.name: Pneumonia
diseases.description: Pneumonia is an infection of one or both lungs that can be caused by bacteria, viruses, or fungi.
symptoms: Chest pain when breathing or coughing
diseases.prevalence: Pneumonia is a common respiratory infection worldwide.

diseases.name: Polio (Poliomyelitis)
diseases.description: Polio is a viral disease that mainly affects children and can cause paralysis. Most infections are asymptomatic.
diagnosis: Laboratory testing of stool or throat samples
diseases.prevalence: Polio has been greatly reduced worldwide but remains a risk where vaccination is low.

diseases.name: Polymyalgia Rheumatica
diseases.description: Polymyalgia rheumatica is an inflammatory disorder that causes muscle pain and stiffness, especially in the shoulders, neck, upper arms, thighs, and hips. Signs and symptoms of polymyalgia rheumatica usually occur quickly — within two weeks. It primarily affects people older than 50.
symptoms: Aches or pain in the shoulders
Aches or pain in the neck, upper arms, buttocks, hips, or thighs
Stiffness in affected areas, particularly in the morning or after being inactive
Pain or stiffness in the wrists, elbows, or knees
treatment: Tocilizumab (if also GCA or relapsing PMR)
diseases.prevalence: About 6 per 10,000 people develop PMR in the US each year. Lifetime risk is about 2% for women and 1% for men.

diseases.name: Polymyositis
diseases.description: Polymyositis is a rare autoimmune disease that causes muscle weakness and inflammation, primarily affecting the muscles closest to the trunk of the body. It can affect your ability to swallow and breathe, and can be associated with other autoimmune conditions. It occurs mostly in adults and is more common in women.
symptoms: Muscle weakness, especially in the hips, thighs, shoulders, upper arms, and neck
causes: Possible viral triggers in genetically susceptible individuals
diseases.prevalence: Rare; affects approximately 1 in 100,000 people.

diseases.name: Polymyositis and Dermatomyositis Overview
diseases.description: Polymyositis and dermatomyositis are two closely related inflammatory muscle diseases (myopathies) in which muscle fiber inflammation leads to progressive muscle weakness. While polymyositis lacks skin involvement, dermatomyositis is distinguished by its characteristic skin rash. Both are autoimmune conditions that can also affect the lungs, heart, and joints.
symptoms: Difficulty rising from a chair, climbing stairs, lifting arms overhead
diseases.prevalence: Combined incidence of approximately 5–10 cases per million per year.

diseases.name: Protein-Energy Malnutrition
diseases.description: Protein-energy malnutrition occurs when the body does not get enough calories and protein to meet its needs.
diseases.prevalence: Undernutrition remains a public health concern in many low- and middle-income regions.

diseases.name: Psoriasis
diseases.description: Psoriasis is a chronic, autoimmune skin disease that speeds up the skin cell life cycle, causing cells to build up rapidly on the surface of the skin. The extra skin cells form scales and red patches that can be itchy and sometimes painful. Psoriasis tends to go through cycles, flaring for a few weeks or months, then subsiding.
symptoms: Red patches of skin covered with thick, silvery scales
Dry, cracked skin that may bleed or itch
causes: Autoimmune — immune system triggers overproduction of skin cells
Triggers: stress, smoking, alcohol, infections, certain medications
diagnosis: Physical examination of skin, scalp, nails
treatment: Topical treatments (corticosteroids, vitamin D analogues, retinoids)
Biologic drugs (TNF-α inhibitors, IL-12/23 inhibitors, IL-17 inhibitors, IL-23 inhibitors)
diseases.prevalence: About 7.5 million Americans are affected by psoriasis.

diseases.name: Psoriatic Arthritis
diseases.description: Psoriatic arthritis is a form of arthritis that affects some people who have psoriasis — a condition that features red patches of skin topped with silvery scales. Most people develop psoriasis first and are later diagnosed with psoriatic arthritis, but the joint problems can sometimes begin before skin patches appear.
symptoms: Nail changes (pitting, separation from nail bed)
treatment: Biologic agents (TNF inhibitors, IL-17 inhibitors, IL-23 inhibitors)
prevention: Treating psoriasis aggressively may reduce PsA risk
diseases.prevalence: Affects approximately 30% of people with psoriasis; about 1 million Americans.

diseases.name: Q Fever
diseases.description: Q fever is a bacterial infection caused by Coxiella burnetii, often linked to exposure to infected livestock or contaminated animal products. It can present as an acute flu-like illness or, in some people, progress to chronic complications.
causes: Exposure to infected cattle, sheep, or goats
Contact with animal birth products or waste
diagnosis: Liver function tests and chest imaging when indicated
treatment: Symptom control with hydration and rest
Monitoring for chronic infection in high-risk patients
Follow-up for cardiac or liver complications
prevention: Maintain animal hygiene and infection control
diseases.prevalence: Q fever is uncommon but reported worldwide, especially in areas with livestock exposure.

diseases.name: Quadrantanopia
diseases.description: Quadrantanopia is a visual field defect where vision is lost in one quarter of the field of view, typically due to damage in the optic pathways of the brain.
symptoms: Loss of vision in one visual quadrant
Frequent bumping into objects on one side
prevention: Control blood pressure, diabetes, and cholesterol
Use protective measures to prevent head injury
Routine follow-up after stroke or brain surgery
diseases.prevalence: Prevalence varies by underlying neurological disorders such as stroke and brain injury.

diseases.name: Rabies
diseases.description: Rabies is a viral disease that affects the central nervous system. It is almost always fatal once symptoms appear, but can be prevented with prompt post-exposure treatment.
symptoms: Fever and pain at bite site
Tingling or burning near the wound
Paralysis and coma in later stages
causes: Bites or scratches from infected animals (most often dogs)
risk_factors: Delay in seeking medical care after exposure
prevention: Avoid animal bites and seek care immediately
diseases.prevalence: Rabies remains a significant public health problem in many parts of Asia and Africa.

diseases.name: Raynaud's Disease (Primary Raynaud's)
diseases.description: Raynaud's disease (primary Raynaud's phenomenon) refers to episodes of reduced blood flow to the fingers (and sometimes toes, ears, nose, and lips) triggered by cold or emotional stress. Unlike secondary Raynaud's (which is associated with another disease), primary Raynaud's occurs without an underlying condition. It is generally less severe than secondary forms.
symptoms: Fingers (and possibly toes) turning white or blue in response to cold or stress
Affected areas turning red when blood flow returns
Numbness, tingling, or stinging pain during episodes
Color changes that occur in phases: white → blue → red
causes: Exaggerated vascular response to cold or emotional stress
No underlying disease (distinguishes primary from secondary)
diagnosis: Blood tests (negative ANA and normal inflammatory markers in primary)
prevention: Wear warm gloves, socks, and layered clothing
diseases.prevalence: Affects approximately 3–5% of the general population; up to 20% in young women.

diseases.name: Raynaud's Phenomenon
diseases.description: Raynaud's phenomenon is a condition that causes some areas of the body — such as fingers and toes — to feel numb and cold in response to cold temperatures or stress. In Raynaud's disease, smaller arteries that supply blood to the skin narrow, limiting blood flow to affected areas. It can be primary (Raynaud's disease) or secondary to another condition.
symptoms: Color changes in skin (white, blue, red) in response to cold or stress
Numbness, prickling, or stinging pain when warming up or stress relief
causes: Secondary causes: scleroderma, lupus, RA, arterial diseases, medications, repetitive trauma
diseases.prevalence: Affects approximately 3–5% of the general population; up to 10% in colder climates.

diseases.name: Reactive Arthritis
diseases.description: Reactive arthritis is joint pain and swelling triggered by an infection in another part of the body, most often the intestines, genitals, or urinary tract. The condition most commonly targets the knees and the joints of the ankles and feet. It is also known as Reiter's syndrome.
symptoms: Skin rash (especially on palms and soles)
causes: Triggered by bacterial infections: Chlamydia, Salmonella, Shigella, Yersinia, Campylobacter
diagnosis: Cultures to identify the triggering infection
diseases.prevalence: Incidence of about 30–40 cases per 100,000 persons per year.

diseases.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
diseases.description: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a chronic inflammatory disorder that can affect more than just the joints. In some people, the condition can damage a wide variety of body systems, including the skin, eyes, lungs, heart, and blood vessels. RA is an autoimmune disease that occurs when the immune system mistakenly attacks the body's own tissues.
symptoms: Joint stiffness that is usually worse in the mornings and after inactivity
Fatigue, fever, and loss of appetite
Organ involvement (lungs, heart, eyes, blood vessels)
causes: Autoimmune disease — immune attacks joint lining (synovium)
diagnosis: Blood tests (RF, anti-CCP antibody, ESR, CRP)
treatment: Biologic DMARDs (TNF inhibitors, IL-6 inhibitors, B-cell/T-cell inhibitors)
prevention: Not smoking is the best-known modifiable risk factor
diseases.prevalence: About 1.3 million Americans have rheumatoid arthritis.

diseases.name: Rotator Cuff Injuries
diseases.description: The rotator cuff is a group of four muscles and their tendons that stabilize the shoulder joint and allow the shoulder to rotate and move. Injuries range from inflammation (tendinitis) and partial tears to complete tears of one or more tendons. Rotator cuff injuries are one of the most common causes of shoulder pain and disability.
symptoms: A dull ache deep in the shoulder
Disturbed sleep, particularly if lying on the affected shoulder
causes: Acute injury (falling on arm, lifting heavy object)
prevention: Proper technique in sports and work
diseases.prevalence: Rotator cuff tears affect approximately 30% of people over age 60; about 2 million people see a doctor annually in the US for rotator cuff issues.

diseases.name: Rotavirus Infection
diseases.description: Rotavirus is a common cause of severe diarrhea and vomiting in infants and young children, which can lead to dehydration.
diseases.prevalence: Rotavirus is a leading cause of severe diarrhea in young children worldwide.

diseases.name: Scleroderma (Systemic Sclerosis)
diseases.description: Scleroderma, also known as systemic sclerosis, is a group of rare diseases that involve the hardening and tightening of the skin and connective tissues. Some types can also harm the blood vessels, internal organs, and the digestive tract. It is an autoimmune disease in which the immune system triggers excessive collagen production.
symptoms: CREST syndrome features (Calcinosis, Raynaud's, Esophageal dysmotility, Sclerodactyly, Telangiectasia)
treatment: No cure; treatment focuses on specific symptoms
Bosentan or sildenafil for pulmonary hypertension
risk_factors: African American and Choctaw Native American ancestry
diseases.prevalence: About 300,000 Americans have scleroderma; it affects 75,000–100,000 adults.

diseases.name: Scoliosis
diseases.description: Scoliosis is a sideways curvature of the spine that occurs most often during the growth spurt just before puberty. While scoliosis can be caused by conditions such as cerebral palsy and muscular dystrophy, the cause of most scoliosis is unknown. Most cases of scoliosis are mild, but some children develop spine deformities that continue to get more severe as they grow.
symptoms: One shoulder blade appears more prominent than the other
One hip higher than the other
One side of the rib cage jutting forward
diagnosis: MRI or CT for complex cases
risk_factors: Sex (girls have higher risk of curve worsening)
prevention: No known prevention for idiopathic scoliosis
diseases.prevalence: Scoliosis affects 2–3% of the population in the United States.

diseases.name: Shin Splints (Medial Tibial Stress Syndrome)
diseases.description: Shin splints (medial tibial stress syndrome) refers to pain along the inner edge of the shinbone (tibia). It is a common overuse injury affecting runners, dancers, and military recruits. Shin splints result from excessive stress on the shinbone and the connective tissues attaching muscles to bone.
symptoms: Tenderness, soreness, or pain along the inner side of the lower leg
Mild swelling of the lower leg
Pain that begins during exercise but may progress to pain at rest
causes: Sudden increase in exercise intensity or duration
Running on hard surfaces or changing surfaces
diagnosis: Bone scan or MRI (for stress fractures)
diseases.prevalence: Affects 13–20% of running injuries; up to 35% of military recruits develop shin splints during training.

diseases.name: Sinusitis
diseases.description: Sinusitis is inflammation of the sinus lining, often following a cold or allergies, leading to facial pain and congestion.
diagnosis: Imaging tests if chronic or severe
diseases.prevalence: Sinusitis is common and often follows upper respiratory infections.

diseases.name: Sjögren's Syndrome
diseases.description: Sjögren's syndrome is a disorder of the immune system identified by its two most common symptoms — dry eyes and a dry mouth. The condition often accompanies other immune system disorders, such as rheumatoid arthritis and lupus. The immune system mistakenly attacks the glands that produce tears and saliva.
symptoms: Dry eyes (feeling of grit in eyes)
diagnosis: Blood tests (ANA, anti-SSA/Ro and anti-SSB/La antibodies, RF)
treatment: Artificial tears and lubricating eye drops
diseases.prevalence: Affects approximately 4 million Americans; third most common rheumatic disease.

diseases.name: Sports Injuries
diseases.description: Sports injuries are injuries that occur during athletic activities or exercise. They can result from accidents, inadequate training, improper use of protective devices, or insufficient warm-up and stretching exercises. The most common sports injuries are sprains and strains, knee injuries, swollen muscles, Achilles tendon injuries, pain along the shin bone, rotator cuff injuries, fractures, and dislocations.
diseases.prevalence: Over 3.5 million children ages 14 and under receive medical treatment for sports injuries annually in the US.

diseases.name: Sprains and Strains
diseases.description: A sprain is a stretching or tearing of ligaments (the tough bands of fibrous tissue that connect bones in a joint). A strain is a stretching or tearing of a muscle or tendon. Both are very common injuries, often occurring during sports or other physical activities. The ankle is the most commonly sprained joint; the back and hamstring are commonly strained.
symptoms: Limited ability to move the affected joint
Hearing or feeling a 'pop' in the joint at time of injury (sprain)
causes: Falls, twisting motions, blunt force trauma (sprains)
treatment: Bracing or casting for severe sprains
Surgery (for complete ligament or tendon tears)
diseases.prevalence: Ankle sprains alone account for about 25,000 injuries per day in the United States.

diseases.name: Still's Disease (Adult-Onset Still's Disease)
diseases.description: Adult-onset Still's disease (AOSD) is a rare form of inflammatory arthritis that features high fevers, rash, and joint pain. It is considered by many to be a spectrum of conditions related to systemic juvenile idiopathic arthritis, but occurring in adults. The condition may be self-limited, intermittent, or chronic.
symptoms: Quotidian fever spikes (high fever once or twice daily)
Salmon-colored skin rash appearing with fever
diagnosis: Blood tests (ferritin markedly elevated, CBC, CRP)
Exclusion of infection, malignancy, and other rheumatic disease
diseases.prevalence: Very rare; approximately 1–34 per million per year.

diseases.name: Stress Fractures
diseases.description: Stress fractures are tiny cracks in a bone caused by repetitive force or overuse, often from jumping or running long distances. They can also develop from normal use of a bone that has been weakened by osteoporosis. Stress fractures most commonly occur in the weight-bearing bones of the lower leg and foot.
symptoms: Pain that develops gradually and worsens with activity
Swelling on the outside of the ankle or foot
Tenderness to touch at the fracture site
causes: Repetitive force exceeding bone's ability to remodel
Bone weakened by osteoporosis or nutritional deficiency
diagnosis: MRI (most sensitive — shows early stress fractures)
treatment: Surgery (for high-risk fractures, e.g., navicular, fifth metatarsal)
diseases.prevalence: Represent 1–20% of all sports medicine injuries; tibial stress fractures account for about 50% of all stress fractures.

diseases.name: Stroke
diseases.description: A stroke happens when blood flow to part of the brain is blocked or a blood vessel breaks, causing brain cells to die quickly.
symptoms: Sudden weakness of face, arm, or leg
treatment: Clot-busting medicines for eligible ischemic strokes
diseases.prevalence: Stroke is a leading cause of disability and death worldwide.

diseases.name: Sunburn-related Illness
diseases.description: Sunburn is skin damage caused by overexposure to ultraviolet (UV) rays from the sun or tanning sources.
symptoms: Fever or chills in severe cases
diseases.prevalence: Sunburn is common with intense sun exposure, especially in summer months.

diseases.name: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE)
diseases.description: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is the most common and severe form of lupus. It is a chronic autoimmune disease in which the immune system produces antibodies that attack the body's own healthy tissue, causing widespread inflammation and damage in multiple organ systems including joints, skin, kidneys, heart, lungs, blood vessels, and brain.
symptoms: Butterfly (malar) rash across cheeks and nose
prevention: Sun protection critical for managing disease
diseases.prevalence: Affects approximately 1.5 million Americans; 5 million worldwide.

diseases.name: Tendinitis
diseases.description: Tendinitis is an inflammation or irritation of a tendon — any one of the thick fibrous cords that attaches muscle to bone. The condition causes pain and tenderness just outside a joint. Tendinitis can occur in any tendon. It is most common around the shoulders, elbows, wrists, knees, and heels.
symptoms: Pain described as a dull ache, especially when moving the affected limb or joint
causes: Some conditions such as diabetes or RA
diseases.prevalence: Tendinitis is extremely common, responsible for about 30% of all soft tissue injuries.

diseases.name: Tendinopathy
diseases.description: Tendinopathy is a broad term encompassing painful conditions occurring in and around tendons in response to overuse. It includes both inflammatory tendinitis and degenerative tendinosis. Common locations include the Achilles tendon, patellar tendon (jumper's knee), rotator cuff, lateral elbow (tennis elbow), and medial elbow (golfer's elbow).
symptoms: Localized tendon pain, especially with activity
Pain that is worse at the start of exercise and improves
causes: Repetitive mechanical loading beyond tissue tolerance
Sudden increase in training volume or intensity
diagnosis: Ultrasound (most useful for diagnosis and monitoring)
treatment: Eccentric and heavy slow resistance exercises
