diseases.prevalence: Extremely common; patellar tendinopathy affects 14% of recreational athletes; Achilles tendinopathy up to 9% of the general population.

diseases.name: Tendon and Ligament Injuries
diseases.description: Tendons connect muscles to bones, while ligaments connect bones to other bones. Both structures can be injured through acute trauma or overuse. Common tendon injuries include tendinitis, tendinosis, and tendon tears. Common ligament injuries include sprains. These injuries are among the most common musculoskeletal problems.
symptoms: Pain at the site of injury
causes: Acute trauma (sudden twist, fall, direct blow)
diseases.prevalence: Among the most common musculoskeletal injuries worldwide; sprains and strains account for millions of ER visits annually.

diseases.name: Tennis Elbow (Lateral Epicondylitis)
diseases.description: Tennis elbow (lateral epicondylitis) is a painful condition caused by overuse of the elbow, particularly the tendons of the forearm muscles that attach to the lateral epicondyle — the bony bump on the outside of the elbow. Despite the name, most cases occur in people who do not play tennis.
symptoms: Pain and tenderness on the outer part of the elbow
Pain that radiates into the forearm
Pain when shaking hands, turning a doorknob, or lifting a cup
Pain that worsens with wrist and hand activities
causes: Repetitive motions of the wrist and arm
prevention: Use appropriate equipment (correct racket grip size)
diseases.prevalence: Affects approximately 1–3% of the population; most common in people aged 30–60.

diseases.name: Thoracic Outlet Syndrome (TOS)
diseases.description: Thoracic outlet syndrome (TOS) is a group of disorders that occur when the blood vessels or nerves in the space between the collarbone and first rib (the thoracic outlet) become compressed. This can cause pain in the shoulders and neck and numbness in the fingers. There are three types: neurogenic (most common), venous, and arterial TOS.
symptoms: Numbness or tingling in the fingers (especially ulnar nerve distribution)
Blood clots in upper extremity veins (Paget-Schroetter syndrome)
causes: Anatomical defects (cervical rib, tight scalene muscles)
treatment: Physical therapy (posture correction, scalene muscle stretching)
Botulinum toxin injections into scalene muscles
Surgery (rib resection, scalenectomy) for refractory or vascular TOS
risk_factors: Trauma to the neck or shoulder
diseases.prevalence: Neurogenic TOS affects approximately 3–80 per 1,000 people; true prevalence uncertain.

diseases.name: Trigger Finger (Stenosing Tenosynovitis)
diseases.description: Trigger finger (stenosing tenosynovitis) is a condition in which one of the fingers gets stuck in a bent position and then snaps straight, like a trigger being pulled and released. It occurs when inflammation narrows the space within the sheath that surrounds the tendon in the affected finger.
symptoms: Finger stiffness, especially in the morning
A popping or clicking sensation when moving the finger
Tenderness or a bump (nodule) at the base of the affected finger
Finger catching or locking in a bent position
Finger locked in bent position (severe cases)
diseases.prevalence: Affects approximately 2–3% of the general population; up to 10% in diabetic patients.

diseases.name: Triple X Syndrome
diseases.description: Triple X syndrome (47,XXX) is a chromosomal condition in females caused by an extra X chromosome. Many individuals have mild symptoms, while others may have developmental or learning challenges.
symptoms: Tall stature in childhood or adolescence
Emotional or behavioral concerns in some cases
causes: Presence of an extra X chromosome (47,XXX)
diagnosis: Prenatal genetic testing in some cases
treatment: Behavioral or psychological support as needed
risk_factors: Random chromosomal errors during cell division
prevention: No known prevention for chromosomal nondisjunction
diseases.prevalence: Estimated to affect around 1 in 1,000 female births, though many cases remain undiagnosed.

diseases.name: Tuberculosis (TB)
diseases.description: Tuberculosis is an infectious disease caused by bacteria that usually affects the lungs and spreads through the air when infected people cough or sneeze.
causes: Airborne spread from an infected person
diagnosis: Rapid molecular tests or sputum tests
risk_factors: Close contact with someone with TB
prevention: BCG vaccination for infants where recommended
diseases.prevalence: TB remains a major public health concern in India and many other countries.

diseases.name: Typhoid Fever
diseases.description: Typhoid fever is a life-threatening infection caused by Salmonella Typhi, usually spread through contaminated food or water.
diagnosis: Blood culture or other lab tests
diseases.prevalence: Typhoid remains common in South Asia and other areas with limited access to safe water.

diseases.name: Undifferentiated Connective Tissue Disease (UCTD)
diseases.description: Undifferentiated connective tissue disease (UCTD) is an autoimmune condition that has features suggestive of a systemic rheumatic disease, but does not fulfill the criteria for a specific defined connective tissue disease such as lupus, scleroderma, or Sjögren's syndrome. Many patients with UCTD remain in this undifferentiated state, while some eventually evolve into a defined disease.
diseases.prevalence: Up to 25% of patients presenting to rheumatology clinics have UCTD.

diseases.name: Vasculitis
diseases.description: Vasculitis is a group of disorders that destroy blood vessels by inflammation. Both arteries and veins can be affected. Lymphangitis is sometimes considered a type of vasculitis. It can affect any blood vessel in the body. Depending on which vessels are affected and the severity of inflammation, vasculitis can range from minor to life-threatening.
symptoms: Organ-specific symptoms depending on which vessels are affected
causes: Can be associated with other conditions: lupus, RA, Hepatitis B or C, drug reactions
prevention: Vaccination against Hepatitis B may prevent one form
diseases.prevalence: Collectively affects about 3 million people in the US. Prevalence varies greatly by type.

diseases.name: Viral Fever
diseases.description: Viral fever is a general term for fever caused by viral infections. It is common and usually self-limited, but can be more severe in vulnerable groups.
causes: Viral infections of the respiratory or gastrointestinal tract
diagnosis: Testing to rule out dengue or malaria if suspected
treatment: Fever control with acetaminophen or ibuprofen
Symptom monitoring and medical review if worsening
prevention: Avoid close contact with sick individuals
Vector control where mosquito-borne viruses are common
diseases.prevalence: Viral fevers are very common, especially during seasonal changes and outbreaks.

diseases.name: Vitamin B12 Deficiency
diseases.description: Vitamin B12 deficiency can lead to anemia and nerve problems due to insufficient B12 intake or absorption.
causes: Poor absorption due to stomach or intestinal problems
diagnosis: Blood test for vitamin B12 levels
diseases.prevalence: Vitamin B12 deficiency is common in older adults and in low-intake diets.

diseases.name: Vitamin D Deficiency
diseases.description: Vitamin D deficiency means the body is not getting enough vitamin D to maintain healthy bones, muscles, and immune function.
diagnosis: Blood test for vitamin D levels
diseases.prevalence: Vitamin D deficiency is common worldwide, including in sunny climates.

diseases.name: Vitiligo
diseases.description: Vitiligo is a long-term skin condition characterized by patches of the skin losing their pigment. The patches of skin affected become white and usually have sharp margins. The hair from the skin may also become white. Vitiligo is caused by a loss of melanocytes, which are cells responsible for skin pigmentation. It can affect people of any race or skin tone but is more visible in people with darker skin.
symptoms: Patchy loss of skin color (usually first appears on sun-exposed areas)
Premature whitening or graying of hair, eyebrows, eyelashes
Loss of color in the tissues that line the inside of the mouth and nose (mucous membranes)
Loss of or change in color of the inner layer of the eye (retina)
causes: Possible triggers: sunburn, emotional stress, exposure to certain chemicals
risk_factors: Personal or family history of autoimmune diseases (thyroid, type 1 diabetes, alopecia areata)
prevention: Sun protection to prevent sunburned vitiligo patches
diseases.prevalence: Affects about 0.5–2% of the global population; approximately 3.5–4 million Americans.

diseases.name: Whooping Cough (Pertussis)
diseases.description: Whooping cough is a highly contagious respiratory infection caused by Bordetella pertussis bacteria.
symptoms: Runny nose and mild cough early on
diagnosis: PCR or lab testing of respiratory samples
diseases.prevalence: Pertussis remains a vaccine-preventable illness with periodic outbreaks.

diseases.name: Wrist Problems and Injuries
diseases.description: Wrist problems include a range of injuries and conditions affecting the bones, tendons, ligaments, and nerves of the wrist joint. Common problems include sprains, fractures (especially of the distal radius), tendinitis, nerve compression (carpal tunnel syndrome), and arthritis. The wrist is particularly vulnerable in falls and repetitive-use activities.
symptoms: Clicking or popping with movement (ligament injuries)
causes: Falls (distal radius fracture — most common wrist fracture)
treatment: Surgery for fractures, ligament tears, or CTS
risk_factors: Age (fractures more common in older adults)
diseases.prevalence: Wrist fractures are among the most common fractures; distal radius fractures account for about 1 in 6 of all fractures treated in emergency rooms.

diseases.name: Xanthelasma
diseases.description: Xanthelasma appears as yellowish cholesterol-rich plaques around the eyelids. It is usually benign but may be associated with lipid abnormalities and cardiovascular risk.
causes: Can occur even with normal lipid levels
diseases.prevalence: Relatively common in adults and may be seen with or without elevated cholesterol.

diseases.name: Xeroderma Pigmentosum
diseases.description: Xeroderma pigmentosum is a rare inherited disorder in which the skin has extreme sensitivity to ultraviolet (UV) light due to impaired DNA repair mechanisms.
symptoms: Severe sun sensitivity from early life
Dry skin and premature skin aging
causes: Inherited mutations in DNA repair genes
diagnosis: Biopsy if suspicious lesions are present
treatment: Surgical management of skin cancers when needed
prevention: Routine skin surveillance and early intervention
diseases.prevalence: Very rare genetic disorder with markedly increased UV-related skin cancer risk.

diseases.name: Xerophthalmia
diseases.description: Xerophthalmia is an eye condition caused by vitamin A deficiency, leading to dryness of the conjunctiva and cornea and potential vision loss if untreated.
treatment: Treatment of corneal complications if present
diseases.prevalence: Still seen in populations with significant vitamin A deficiency and malnutrition.

diseases.name: XYY Syndrome
diseases.description: XYY syndrome is a chromosomal condition in males caused by an extra Y chromosome (47,XYY). Many individuals are mildly affected, but some have learning and behavioral difficulties.
symptoms: Behavioral or attention concerns in some children
risk_factors: Random chromosomal error during sperm formation
prevention: Early diagnosis and developmental support improve outcomes
diseases.prevalence: Estimated around 1 in 1,000 male births, with many undiagnosed cases.

diseases.name: Yaws
diseases.description: Yaws is a chronic bacterial infection caused by Treponema pallidum pertenue, primarily affecting skin, bones, and cartilage in children living in tropical regions.
causes: Direct skin contact with infectious lesions
Poor sanitation and crowded living conditions
diagnosis: Clinical examination of skin and bone findings
treatment: Treatment of close contacts in outbreaks
risk_factors: Poor hygiene and limited healthcare access
diseases.prevalence: Occurs mainly in tropical low-resource settings and is a target of global eradication efforts.

diseases.name: Yellow Fever
diseases.description: Yellow fever is a viral hemorrhagic disease transmitted by infected mosquitoes in tropical regions of Africa and South America.
symptoms: Bleeding and organ failure in critical illness
causes: Transmission by infected Aedes or Haemagogus mosquitoes
Travel to endemic areas without vaccination
treatment: Monitoring for bleeding and organ dysfunction
diseases.prevalence: Endemic in parts of Africa and South America with periodic outbreaks.

diseases.name: Yellow Nail Syndrome
diseases.description: Yellow nail syndrome is a rare disorder characterized by yellow thickened nails, lymphedema, and respiratory manifestations such as chronic cough or pleural effusion.
causes: Possible association with autoimmune or respiratory disorders
diagnosis: Clinical diagnosis based on triad of signs
prevention: Early management of lymphedema and lung issues
diseases.prevalence: Very rare syndrome with limited epidemiological data.

diseases.name: Zellweger Syndrome
diseases.description: Zellweger syndrome is a severe peroxisome biogenesis disorder present from birth, affecting multiple organs including brain, liver, and kidneys.
treatment: Monitoring and treatment of organ complications
prevention: Early supportive care planning after diagnosis
diseases.prevalence: Rare inherited disorder within the Zellweger spectrum.

diseases.name: Zenkers Diverticulum
diseases.description: Zenker’s diverticulum is an outpouching of the upper esophagus that can cause swallowing problems and regurgitation, typically in older adults.
causes: Increased pressure at upper esophageal sphincter
treatment: Open surgical repair for selected patients
prevention: Prompt treatment to reduce aspiration risk
diseases.prevalence: Uncommon and more frequently diagnosed in older adults.

diseases.name: Zika Virus Infection
diseases.description: Zika virus infection is a mosquito-borne viral illness. Most cases are mild, but infection during pregnancy is associated with serious fetal complications.
causes: Zika virus transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes
diagnosis: PCR testing in blood/urine during acute illness
treatment: Avoid NSAIDs until dengue is excluded
risk_factors: Residence or travel in endemic regions
diseases.prevalence: Occurs in outbreaks in tropical and subtropical regions.

diseases.name: Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome
diseases.description: Zollinger-Ellison syndrome is a condition in which gastrin-secreting tumors (gastrinomas) cause excessive stomach acid production and severe recurrent peptic ulcers.
symptoms: Nausea or gastrointestinal bleeding in severe cases
causes: Gastrinoma tumors in pancreas or duodenum
Association with MEN1 syndrome in some patients
treatment: Management of metastatic disease when present
prevention: Early screening in high-risk MEN1 families
Prompt treatment to prevent ulcer complications
diseases.prevalence: Rare endocrine-gastrointestinal disorder.

diseases.name: Zoonotic Diseases
diseases.description: Zoonotic diseases are infections transmitted from animals to humans through direct contact, vectors, food, water, or environmental exposure.
causes: Bacterial, viral, parasitic, or fungal pathogens
Animal bites or contact with infected animals
diagnosis: Targeted laboratory testing by pathogen type
Imaging or specialist consultation when required
treatment: Public health reporting for outbreak control
risk_factors: Poor sanitation and unsafe food handling
prevention: Safe food preparation and water practices
diseases.prevalence: Zoonotic infections are globally common and represent a major public health burden.
